headlines.geopolitics: Iranian Military Shows It Knows How to Adapt, U.S. Officials Say
headlines.economy: Culture at Columbia Shielded Sexual Assault by Physician, Report Finds
headlines.domestic: Fox News Poll: Voters expect AI to transform our lives — but today is not that day

metadata.daily_do: Check your AI detection skills with one piece of content today — news article, social media post, or video. See if you can spot what's real.

clips.caption: When Dr. Pringle corrects everyone's AI assumptions
Jax explains why office chairs are now a national security issue
Duke's take on why AI polls don't matter

script.text: The internet revolution? Complete nonsense. This is nothing like the 1990s, and everyone who keeps saying it is has learned exactly the wrong lesson from history.

Monday, March 10th. I'm Leo Barros, and this is The Friction. Let's get you caught up. First, geopolitics — Iran's military is showing new tactical adaptability, specifically targeting what U.S. officials call American vulnerabilities in the region.
Here we go.
They're focusing on air defense systems that protect our troops and assets, suggesting they've shifted strategy from broad confrontation to precision targeting.
That's... actually smart. Terrifying, but smart.

Second, economy — Columbia University and NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital are dealing with fallout from an external report about sexual assault allegations against physician Robert Hadden. Two doctors have stepped down after the report found administrators mishandled the situation.
Oh man, not another institution covering for predators.
And domestic — a new Fox News poll shows 87% of voters expect AI to transform American life, but only 47% find it useful right now. There's a huge demographic divide on this.
Let me guess — young people love it, old people hate it, and everyone's pretending they understand it.
The income divide is what gets me. Lower-income households are most skeptical, which makes sense if you can't afford the latest tech.
We'll dig into all of this, plus Professor Pringle has thoughts on why everyone's getting the AI comparison wrong.
Indeed I do.
Let's start with Iran. Duke, you've been tracking this — what does tactical adaptability actually mean here?
It means they've been watching how we operate and they found our weak spots. Air defense systems are expensive, complex, and if you take one out, you've created a gap that's hard to fill quickly.
But this is exactly the escalation cycle that gets us into bigger conflicts. They probe, we reinforce, they probe harder, we reinforce more, and suddenly we're in a shooting war.
Or — hear me out — we show them that targeting our defenses has consequences, and they stop doing it.
How's that working out so far? We've been playing deterrence games with Iran for, what, forty years?
And they haven't directly attacked the homeland in forty years, so... pretty well?
I love how we're debating the effectiveness of a strategy that's older than I am.
The question is whether this represents a shift in Iranian strategy or just better intelligence on their part.
It's both, and that's what makes it dangerous. They're not just throwing darts at a map anymore — they're studying our deployment patterns, our response times, our logistics chains.
Which is why you don't give them time to study. You move fast, you move unpredictably, and you make sure the cost of probing is higher than the value of the intelligence.
Or — and this is crazy — we could reduce our footprint so there are fewer targets to study in the first place.
Right, because Iran definitely won't fill that vacuum with their own proxies. That's worked great in Iraq and Syria.
What's interesting is that U.S. officials are acknowledging this publicly. That suggests either they want Iran to know we know, or they're preparing public opinion for a response.
Or they're trying to justify more defense spending. Sorry, that was cynical, but also probably true.
Sometimes cynical and true are the same thing. If Iran's getting better at targeting our stuff, we need better stuff.
Can we just appreciate that we're having a rational debate about military strategy without anyone screaming about World War Three? I feel like we've grown as a show.
The bar is very low, Jax. But you're not wrong.
I just think we need to be honest about what deterrence actually costs. It's not just money — it's diplomatic flexibility, regional relationships, and the constant risk of miscalculation.
And the cost of not deterring is Iran with nuclear weapons and regional hegemony. I'll take the current costs.
We'll see how this plays out. The fact that they're being so systematic about it suggests this isn't going away.
Professor Pringle, you mentioned earlier that everyone's getting the AI comparison wrong. What are we missing?

Well Leo, everyone keeps comparing AI adoption to the internet revolution of the 1990s, and it's complete nonsense. This is nothing like the internet, and everyone who keeps saying it is has learned exactly the wrong lesson from history.
Oh boy, here we go.

The internet was infrastructure. Like the railroad or the telegraph — it connected existing things in new ways. AI is not infrastructure. It's automation. The proper comparison is not the 1990s internet. It's the 1970s calculator.
Wait, the calculator?

In 1970, if you needed to do complex mathematics, you hired a mathematician. By 1980, you bought a calculator for twelve dollars. The mathematician didn't disappear — but the profession fundamentally changed.
So you're saying AI is going to make certain jobs obsolete, not create new industries.
I'm saying it's going to make certain types of thinking obsolete, which is far more profound. The internet made information easier to access. AI makes thinking unnecessary in specific domains.
And the public reaction? This poll shows people expect transformation but don't find it useful yet.
That's exactly what happened with calculators. In 1975, people said 'Why would I need this? I can do arithmetic.' By 1985, they couldn't imagine living without one.
I literally cannot do long division anymore. Like, I know it exists, but if you put a gun to my head and asked me to divide 847 by 23, I would die.

Precisely. And that's what's coming for writing, for analysis, for pattern recognition. The concern isn't that AI will take jobs — it's that it will take cognitive abilities we don't realize we're losing until they're gone.
That's actually terrifying. The income divide in the poll makes more sense now — if you can't afford the latest AI tools, you're not just behind technologically, you're behind cognitively.
But that assumes everyone needs to be doing high-level cognitive work. Most jobs are still physical, still require human judgment, still need people to show up and solve problems in real time.

That's true, but it misses the broader point. When calculators became ubiquitous, we stopped teaching children to do arithmetic by hand. What happens when AI becomes ubiquitous and we stop teaching children to think critically?
The poll shows 89% want disclosure when content is AI-generated. That suggests people understand there's a trust issue.

Yes, but disclosure only works if people can still tell the difference. Once AI becomes sophisticated enough, disclosure becomes the only way to know — and disclosure can be gamed, ignored, or simply forgotten over time.
So basically we're all going to become cognitive infants who can't think without our AI pacifiers.
Not infants — specialists. We'll become very good at using AI tools and very bad at the underlying skills those tools replace. Whether that's progress or decline depends on your perspective.

Alright, let's move to the weird news because honestly, today's stories are perfectly designed to make everyone feel slightly uncomfortable about the future. First up — Palantir, the company that builds AI weapons systems for the Pentagon, has deployed their technology for a new mission: finding office chairs for government workers.
I'm sorry, what?

Apparently the government has so much office furniture spread across so many buildings that they needed AI to figure out where all the chairs are. Because nothing says 'peak bureaucracy' like using military-grade artificial intelligence to locate seating.
This is actually a perfect example of what Doc was talking about. We're using AI for inventory management because we've lost the ability to just... keep track of our stuff.
Although to be fair, if you're going to use AI for something, office chair logistics seems less dystopian than targeted killings.
True, but imagine being a Palantir engineer. You got into this to help fight terrorism, and now you're debugging code that can't find the Herman Miller chair from Accounting.
Hey, that Herman Miller chair probably cost more than my truck. I'd want to keep track of it too.
Speaking of government efficiency, Louisiana schools have until Friday to post the Ten Commandments in every classroom. This is happening. In 2026. In public schools.
I thought we settled this in like 1962.
Look, I'm not saying it's constitutional, but at least it's better moral guidance than TikTok.

The best part is they have to figure out which version to use. There are like three different versions of the Ten Commandments depending on which tradition you follow. So now Louisiana school administrators are doing Biblical scholarship.
This happened in Kentucky in the 1980s. The Supreme Court ruled it unconstitutional in about fifteen minutes.
That was a different Supreme Court, Professor.
Ah. Yes. Quite right.
In Texas election news, there's a candidate for mayor whose legal name is literally 'Literally Anybody Else.' Like, that's on the ballot. 'Literally Anybody Else for Mayor.'
Honestly? That candidate might have my vote. At least they're being honest about what they represent.
This is what happens when people lose faith in institutions. Democracy becomes performance art.
Or maybe democracy was always performance art and we're just being more honest about it.
That's... surprisingly profound, Jax.

I have my moments. And finally, in Florida — because of course it's Florida — a drug dealer locked a customer in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt. The customer had to be rescued by police.
That's not drug dealing, that's kidnapping with extra steps.
The fact that this happened in a storage unit is somehow the most Florida part of the story.
Right? Like, even Florida criminals are dealing with the housing crisis. 'Sorry bro, I can't afford a proper dungeon, but I've got a ten-by-ten at Public Storage.'
And on that deeply disturbing note...
Your Daily Do: Professor Pringle's calculator comparison got me thinking. We're all confident we can spot AI-generated content, but are we actually good at it?
Oh, I love this. Like a test?
Exactly. Today, find one piece of content — could be a news article, a social media post, a video — and really examine it. Ask yourself: is this AI-generated? Then try to verify your answer.
This is going to make me paranoid about everything I read.
Good! Paranoia is the appropriate response to living in the future.
The point isn't to become paranoid — it's to become calibrated. Right now, we think we're better at spotting AI than we probably are.
As Santayana said, 'Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it.' But those who cannot distinguish the present from the artificial are condemned to live in neither.
Did Santayana actually say that second part?
No, I added that. But he would have, if he'd lived to see ChatGPT.

And that's The Friction for Monday, March 10th. I'm Jax Mooney, reminding you that if you can't tell whether this show was written by humans or AI, the future is already here and it's mildly concerning. We'll see you tomorrow.
